data.title: SentinelOne outlines $998M–$1.02B FY26 revenue target as AI and platform adoption accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SentinelOne (S) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Tomer Weingarten indicated that "Q2 was a landmark quarter for SentinelOne. Our total ARR grew 24% and crossed $1 billion, a significant milestone in our growth journey." He highlighted record second quarter net new ARR, broad-based performance with strong customer additions, and increased adoption of AI and data solutions.
  • Weingarten announced the launch of SentinelOne Flex, describing it as "a unified flexible licensing model that gives organizations the agility to manage entitlements, seamlessly deploy new platform capabilities and drive greater return from their security investments." The Flex model has already delivered an 8-figure total deal value and is enabling larger, more efficient deals.
  • He also referenced the acquisition of Prompt Security, describing it as "a strategic step forward for us in one of the fastest emerging and most urgent domains in cybersecurity," targeting generative AI security and data leakage prevention.
  • CFO Barbara A. Larson stated, "Our total ARR grew 24% and surpassed $1 billion, a significant milestone in our growth journey. We added net new ARR of $53 million in the quarter, which significantly exceeded our expectations." She noted that revenue grew 22% year-over-year to $242 million, international markets represented 38% of total revenue, and operating margin improved by more than 500 basis points year-over-year.

Outlook

  • Larson provided guidance for Q3 revenue of approximately $256 million, reflecting 22% year-over-year growth. For fiscal year 2026, she stated, "we now expect revenue in the range of $998 million to $1.02 billion, $1 billion at the midpoint, representing 22% year-over-year growth."
  • She expects Q3 gross margin to be approximately 78.5% and for the full year, gross margin between 78.5% and 79%. Q3 operating margin is expected to be about 4% and full year operating margin about 3%.
  • Larson reiterated, "we are reaffirming our commitment to delivering positive free cash flow for the full year, which we expect to be a few points higher than operating margin."

Financial Results

  • SentinelOne reported total ARR surpassing $1 billion, net new ARR of $53 million, and revenue of $242 million for Q2.
  • International revenue grew 27% and constituted 38% of total revenue. Customers with ARR of $100,000 or more grew 23% to 1,513. The company maintained a gross margin of 79% and achieved an operating profitability of 2% in Q2. Net income margin increased to 5%.
  • Remaining performance obligations grew 26% to $1.2 billion. Free cash flow margin on a trailing 12-month basis was 2%, with $1.2 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and investments and no debt at quarter-end.
  • The purchase price for Prompt Security was approximately $180 million. Larson noted the acquisition's top line impact would be minimal in fiscal year 2026 with an estimated 80 basis point impact to full year operating margin.

Q&A

  • John Stephen DiFucci, Guggenheim: Asked about drivers of expansion and metrics to gauge platform adoption. Weingarten explained Q2 was "really broad-based with strong contributions both from new logos and expansion with existing customers," adding that "Purple AI and data, they remain our fastest-growing solutions."
  • Robbie David Owens, Piper Sandler: Sought clarity on net new ARR trends for Q3. Larson responded, "We don't provide formal ARR guidance, but you can approximate it just based on our revenue outlook...Q2 results and the updated revenue outlook implies a relatively improved view on net new ARR for the full year."
  • Brad Alan Zelnick, Deutsche Bank: Asked about Flex and its financial implications. Weingarten described customer flexibility and increased module consumption, while Larson stated Flex will "be very similar to our other ARR contracts, which is TCV divided by duration and revenue will be recognized ratably over the duration of the contract."
  • Joseph Anthony Gallo, Jefferies: Inquired about Prompt Security's revenue contribution. Weingarten described Prompt as addressing "a new class of risk" in generative AI and expressed expectation for significant contribution "in years to come."
  • Multiple analysts focused on drivers behind improved performance, Flex adoption, the impact of macro conditions, and margin outlook. Larson consistently mentioned maintaining prudent assumptions for the second half and taking a measured approach to guidance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a slightly positive to neutral tone, acknowledging improved momentum but pressing for specifics on ARR, revenue outlook, and the impact of new initiatives. Questions often focused on sustainability of growth and Flex's future impact.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in prepared remarks, highlighting milestones and product adoption. During Q&A, their tone was measured and occasionally cautious, particularly when discussing guidance and macroeconomic factors. Phrases such as "we're being prudent" and "we're excited about the early momentum" were common.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts displayed improved optimism, with less focus on macro-driven caution and more emphasis on product-driven growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw the introduction of the Flex licensing model and the Prompt Security acquisition, both strategic shifts not present last quarter.
  • Guidance language remains prudent, but revenue outlook for the second half was raised, contrasting with the previous quarter's more cautious stance.
  • Management's tone shifted from a defensive explanation of macro impacts last quarter to a more confident focus on execution and innovation this quarter. Analysts' tone moved from skepticism about ARR trends to interest in the scalability of new offerings.
  • Key metrics such as ARR, revenue, and margin all improved compared to Q1, with stronger expansion, record ARR, and operating profitability achieved.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted continued macroeconomic uncertainty, which "can influence deal timing and sales cycles."
  • Potential variability in the timing of larger deals was factored into guidance.
  • The top line impact from the Prompt Security acquisition is expected to be minimal in FY26, with an 80 basis point operating margin impact.
  • Analysts questioned the prudence of raised guidance given strong Q2 results, seeking reassurance around sustainability.

Final Takeaway

SentinelOne management emphasized that Q2 marked a significant step forward, with ARR surpassing $1 billion and strong adoption of both new and existing product suites. The company launched its Flex licensing model, closed an acquisition in the emerging GenAI security space, and raised its full-year revenue outlook to a midpoint of $1 billion. Management remains focused on delivering durable growth and operating leverage while acknowledging macroeconomic uncertainties and maintaining prudent guidance for the second half of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Affirm signals ongoing acceleration in GMV and cardholder spend, eyes further growth through international expansion and AI-driven products
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Affirm Holdings, Inc. (AFRM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Max Roth Levchin stated, "we actually set a new record in most of our metrics, which is unusual. Fiscal Q2 is a normal peak but this is Q4 and yes, it is the record. So that's really cool. I should tell you that our growth is accelerating and we are firing on all pistons." Levchin highlighted the team's tenure and affirmed his confidence in the company's trajectory.
  • Levchin underscored the ongoing expansion of Affirm Card, noting, "the current trailing 12 months of the cardholder is about $4,700. So I think the last time we dropped this number, it was along the lines of $3,500. This is across all Affirm services, so this is card and in all the other places where you might go, but card dominates that spend obviously. So we're not quite at the $7,500 but we're more than halfway there."
  • On new market initiatives, Levchin shared that Affirm is "in friends-and-family testing in the U.K. with our Shopify friends. It's very exciting so that's obviously an enormous potential of that is not lost on anyone."
  • COO Michael Linford commented on capital partnerships, "we partner with who we think is the blue chips of these asset managers. And that can come in the form of large strategic partnerships with world-class investors like Sixth Street or very good insurance asset managers up and down our stack."
  • CFO Robert W. O'Hare added, "given the short duration of the loans that we're originating, the most important thing for us is that we have a full picture of the borrower's wherewithal to repay the loan at the time of origination. And then the asset is so short-dated and we're increasingly working with consumers that we've seen before. 95% of our transactions came from repeat borrowers this quarter."

Outlook

  • Affirm leadership maintained a positive outlook on both growth and profitability, with Levchin stating, "from the consumer point of view... we think that it continues to perform. It's really maybe a commentary on how strong the momentum is in the U.S., and to at least a similar degree, Canadian consumer and soon, we'll find out what that looks like for U.K. one."
  • The company anticipates continued acceleration in GMV and further expansion of the 0% APR program, with Levchin noting, "monthly 0% loans were growing north of 90% year-on-year. So we would expect that, that loan product in particular, continues to take a bit of share within our mix."
  • The international rollout, particularly in the U.K. with Shopify, is positioned as a near-term growth lever. Levchin indicated, "as we scale Shopify, that is absolutely subject to change just based on what this will do relative to what's available."

Financial Results

  • Levchin described Q4 as a record-setting quarter for most metrics.
  • O'Hare reported that "95% of our transactions came from repeat borrowers this quarter," highlighting strong customer engagement and low risk for new originations.
  • Affirm Card volume reached $1.2 billion with a 10% attach rate, and the trailing 12-month cardholder spend increased to $4,700 across Affirm services.
  • Monthly 0% APR loans grew more than 90% year-on-year, and the number of merchants funding the offering doubled to 7% of the merchant base.

Q&A

  • Dan Dolev, Mizuho: Asked about improved optimism versus last quarter and drivers for the uptick. Levchin responded that demand for service and credit performance remain strong, and originations are accelerating.
  • Daniel Rock Perlin, RBC: Queried repeat rates for first-time 0% APR users. Levchin replied, "there's not a tremendous difference in terms of repeat of users that have been acquired through 0s or not. But... do 0% users flip over to interest bearing? And they do."
  • Adam Frisch, Evercore ISI: Questioned consumer risk and FICO distribution amid potential headwinds. Levchin explained the company is "not concerned" due to real-time underwriting and ongoing credit monitoring, emphasizing that "credit is job #1."
  • Will Nance, Goldman Sachs: Asked about risk from alternative lenders with increased funding. Linford stressed Affirm's strategy of choosing "blue chips of these asset managers" and maintaining long-term discipline.
  • Multiple analysts, including Adib Choudhury (William Blair), pressed on international and U.K. growth. Levchin confirmed the U.K. launch is underway, with early testing and plans to leverage Shopify for rapid scaling.
  • Rob Wildhack, Autonomous Research: Inquired about the 0% APR strategy. Levchin attributed Affirm's success to underwriting strength and the ability to structure merchant-subsidized offers profitably.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally positive, frequently using phrases like "crushing it" and "great results," but their questions often probed sustainability of current trends, competitive risks, and consumer health.
  • Management displayed high confidence, using phrases such as "we are firing on all pistons," and providing detailed, assertive responses. Levchin often emphasized control over credit risk and growth levers.
  • The tone remained highly confident in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with management maintaining more optimism this quarter compared to last, when there was more caution around macro risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 featured more assertive claims of record results and accelerating growth, compared to Q3's more cautious optimism and focus on judicious growth.
  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize rapid expansion, especially in 0% APR products and international markets.
  • The focus of analyst questions evolved from managing risk and competition (previous quarter) to probing Affirm's ability to sustain growth and capitalize on new markets and partnerships.
  • Management's confidence in credit quality and underwriting remains strong, but this quarter, the emphasis shifted to growth acceleration and the scaling of the Affirm Card.
  • The share of repeat borrowers and engagement with new products (especially the card) were more frequently highlighted.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing monitoring of macroeconomic shifts, including student loan repayments and consumer credit health.
  • Affirm continues to prioritize credit risk management, with Levchin reaffirming, "credit is job #1." The team is prepared to adjust underwriting standards as needed.
  • Analyst concerns centered on competitive pressures from alternative lenders, risk in funding markets, and the sustainability of high growth in 0% APR and card products.
  • The upcoming transition of a major enterprise merchant was addressed, with the outlook assuming "the integration goes away at the end of this quarter" and volume from that partner to be conservatively modeled.

Final Takeaway

Affirm Holdings delivered a record-setting quarter, highlighted by accelerating growth in GMV, substantial increases in Affirm Card usage, and the rapid expansion of its 0% APR product. Management emphasized the strength of its underwriting, the durability of consumer demand, and the company’s readiness to capitalize on international opportunities, particularly in the U.K. with Shopify. Repeat usage remains high, and new product initiatives, including AI-driven checkout optimization, signal a focus on both innovation and disciplined risk management as Affirm moves into its next phase of growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Caterpillar raises expected impact of tariffs, says full-year costs could hit $1.8B
data.description:
Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT) -2.7% post-market Thursday after disclosing it now expects the net impact from tariffs in the $1.5B-$1.8B range this year, up from a previous estimate of $1.3B-$1.5B, as well as a $500M-$600M impact in Q3, compared to its earlier outlook for $400M-$500M.

Including the net impact from incremental tariffs, Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT) said in an 8-K filing that it now expects its full-year adjusted operating profit margin to come in near the bottom of the target margin range, although it left its full-year sales outlook unchanged.

"While the company continues to take initial mitigating actions to reduce this impact, trade and tariff negotiations continue to be fluid," Caterpillar (CAT) said.

data.title: India poised to increase Russian energy imports, defying U.S. demands
data.description:
The Trump administration's steep tariffs on India have not forced the country to stop buying Russian oil, as Reuters reported Thursday that Indian imports of Russian oil are expected to rise next month.

Indian refiners plan to increase their purchases of Russian oil in September by 10%-20% compared to August levels, or by an additional 150K-300K bbl/day, according to Reuters, which cited trading sources involved in the Indian market.

India reportedly imported 1.5M bbl/day of oil from Russia during the first 20 days of August, unchanged from July and slightly below the average of 1.6M bbl/day in the January-June period; the volumes equal 1.5% of global supply, making India the largest buyer of seaborne Russian crude, which covers ~40% of India's oil needs.

India likely will remain a major buyer of Russian oil despite 50% U.S. tariffs on Indian imports that started Wednesday, Commonwealth Bank of Australia analyst Vivek Dhar said in a note.

Although ship tracking data indicates Russian exports to India have eased by 15%‑20% since late June, the lack of an official ban or reduction of purchases reflects "the political tightrope India is trying to walk," Dhar wrote. "India is trying to make some concessions to U.S. demands, while also trying to keep its relationship with Russia intact."

India's oil refineries likely will largely continue to purchase Russian oil to maximize profits as it trades at a slight discount to Brent oil, Dhar said.

Crude oil futures settled higher Thursday, with the market watching increased drone strikes in the Russia-Ukraine war just weeks after hopes were raised for peace talks.

German Chancellor Merz told reporters that a meeting between Ukraine's President Zelenskiy and Russia's President Putin "won't happen."

Meanwhile, Citi maintained its average Brent price forecast at $66/bbl for Q3 and$63/bbl in Q4 as "oversupply should start to exert greater impact on the oil supply-demand balance into year end."

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for October delivery finished +0.7% to $64.60/bbl, front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for October delivery closed +0.8% to $68.62/bbl, and front-month Nymex natural gas for October - the new front month - ended +2% to $2.944/MMBtu, its third straight daily gain.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (NYSEARCA:XLE)

data.title: Petco raises 2025 adjusted EBITDA outlook to $385M-$395M with continued margin expansion amid transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Petco Health and Wellness Company, Inc. (WOOF) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Joel D. Anderson, CEO, emphasized "the progress we are making in strengthening the foundation of our operating model and improving our retail fundamentals while positioning Petco for sustainable, profitable growth." Anderson highlighted that Petco is "continuing our transformation journey and pleased we have the confidence to raise our earnings outlook." He cited a meaningful improvement in profitability, with "operating income by over $40 million and generating more than $50 million in free cash flow" for the quarter, and stated, "we delivered $114 million in adjusted EBITDA."
  • Anderson described the relaunch of the "Where the Pets Go" tagline and new in-store experiences, noting, "Initial results indicate it is resonating with strong feedback and positive reactions from our customers." He added, "we have seen positive customer sentiment and engagement around these events on our social channels and sequential increases in our NPS score since the end of last year."
  • Anderson discussed strategic marketing, merchandise overhaul, and a new product category aimed at human customers, stating, "Products are designed to be giftable, affordable and impulse worthy, celebrating the bond between pets and the people who love them."
  • Anderson stated, "We recently welcomed a new leader for our e-commerce channel, and he has already identified several opportunities."
  • Sabrina Louise Simmons, CFO, stated, "In line with our outlook, net sales were down 2.3% with comparable sales down 1.4%." She emphasized, "gross margin expansion of more than 120 basis points versus last year to 39.3%, with gross margin in both products and services expanding once again this quarter." Simmons highlighted, "SG&A decreased $36 million below last year and leveraged more than 150 basis points."
  • Simmons reported, "operating profit to $43 million. Adjusted EBITDA increased $30 million to $114 million and expanded nearly 220 basis points to 7.6% as a percent of sales."

Outlook

  • Simmons raised the adjusted EBITDA outlook for 2025, stating, "We now expect adjusted EBITDA to be between $385 million and $395 million, an increase of roughly 16% at the midpoint."
  • The company continues to expect net sales for the year to be down low single digits, reflecting store closures.
  • For Q3, Simmons said, "we expect adjusted EBITDA to be between $92 million and $94 million, up nearly 15% year-over-year at the midpoint," and "net sales to be down low single digits versus the prior year."
  • The company expects depreciation of about $200 million, net interest expense of approximately $130 million, and $125 million to $130 million in capital expenditures for the year.

Financial Results

  • Net sales were down 2.3% with comparable sales down 1.4%.
  • Gross margin expanded more than 120 basis points to 39.3%.
  • SG&A decreased $36 million below last year and leveraged more than 150 basis points.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $114 million, expanding nearly 220 basis points to 7.6% as a percent of sales.
  • Operating profit increased $41 million year-over-year to $43 million.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was over $50 million and year-to-date about $10 million.
  • Cash balance at quarter-end was $190 million, with total liquidity of $684 million.
  • Inventory ended 9.5% below last year.

Q&A

  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Asked when to expect positive comp. Anderson indicated, "the results will begin to show up in 2026 as it relates to a positive comp, but we will start to work on ideas right now."
  • Lasser also asked about gross margin gains and online segment weakness. Anderson replied, "We've really been focused on the stores. It is where the majority of our sales are... the traction we're getting in e-comm is working as well, but that's been more focused on the bottom line." Simmons added, "online last year did have much more promo and stacking that we needed to clean up."
  • Steven Zaccone, Citi: Requested more detail on gross margin and tariffs. Simmons explained, "We've been working every lever AUR, AUC... for sure, tariffs start to impact. So we had almost no tariff impact in Q2... as we go to Q3, it becomes meaningful, and then it becomes even much more meaningful in the fourth quarter."
  • Zaccone followed up on mitigation efforts and pricing. Simmons said, "We've been doing pricing all year long... will we be using that lever? Of course, we will, but it will be with a consumer-first lens."
  • Jacob Nivasch, Guggenheim: Inquired about planogram resets and the North Star initiative. Anderson described completed dog and cat resets, adding, "You'll continue to see more of those as we move through this year and into next year." On North Star, Anderson outlined, "four main pillars... delivering amazing store experience... services at scale... merchandising differentiation... winning with omnichannel."
  • Kaumil Gajrawala, Jefferies: Asked about e-commerce retool and inventory. Anderson said e-comm work is ongoing, with a new leader implementing improvements. Simmons noted inventory management success but emphasized continuous improvement.
  • Kendall Toscano, BofA: Queried about comp drivers. Simmons responded, "Transactions is a place we are very focused on improving."
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Sought insight on pet families and merchandise mix. Anderson noted, "industry data is showing that the pet space is relatively flat right now," and observed many one-time customers, with an opportunity to grow repeat business.
  • Justin Kleber, Baird: Asked about comp drag from promo cleanup and Q4 EBITDA guidance. Anderson did not specify a comp impact but stated, "as we continue to find opportunities where there were empty calorie sales, we are offsetting those with some of the growth initiatives." Simmons attributed Q4 guidance to tariffs, investment opportunities, and macro volatility.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on timing for positive comps, gross margin drivers, and risk from tariffs, with a slightly positive but probing tone, seeking details on execution and strategy. For example, Michael Lasser asked, "when is a reasonable expectation for us to hold the firm accountable for generating a positive comp?"
  • Management maintained a confident and detailed tone in prepared remarks, shifting to cautious optimism and transparency in Q&A, frequently referencing ongoing work and future opportunities. Anderson stated, "We are harvesting that cultural heritage with an equal focus on operating disciplines and a winning mindset."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remains slightly positive but more focused on execution gaps, while management's confidence is higher, supported by improved results and guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for adjusted EBITDA was raised from a previous range of $375 million-$390 million to $385 million-$395 million.
  • Net sales and comparable sales remain negative, with continued store closures impacting top-line results, similar to Q1.
  • Gross margin expansion accelerated, with Q2 reaching 39.3% compared to 38.2% in Q1.
  • Management tone is more assertive, highlighting transformation progress and cultural alignment, while analysts increased focus on margin sustainability, tariff risks, and timing for sales growth.
  • Strategic focus is shifting from foundational improvements to reinvestment and preparing for Phase 3 growth, while maintaining operational discipline.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariffs are identified as a mounting headwind, with Simmons noting, "tariffs will become sequentially more meaningful as we move through the back half."
  • The company recognizes macroeconomic volatility and is keeping "dry powder" for potential investments or to guard against uncertainty.
  • Execution gaps remain in transactions and digital, with management emphasizing ongoing initiatives and leadership changes to address these areas.
  • Inventory and new merchandise introduction are being tightly managed to avoid markdown risk, with Simmons stating, "those buys will be tight and well controlled and seasonal and fast turning."

Final Takeaway

Petco management highlighted significant progress in operational discipline and profitability, leading to a raised adjusted EBITDA outlook for 2025 and continued gross margin expansion. The company remains focused on transformation, addressing execution gaps in transactions and digital, and preparing for a return to growth with targeted reinvestment and strategic initiatives across marketing, merchandising, and omnichannel capabilities. Tariffs and macro uncertainty remain key risks, but leadership expresses confidence in the foundation built and the roadmap for sustainable, profitable growth ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exxon says global net zero goals fading as coal use climbs
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) said the global energy sector’s net zero goals likely will slip further past 2050 due to high energy costs and a rebound in the demand for coal, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

In its newly published Global Outlook, Exxon (NYSE:XOM) forecast emissions will fall by 25% by 2050, well short of the two-thirds drop needed to meet a target set by the United Nations' Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, while coal consumption is rising because of a slowdown in the deployment of renewable energy sources and growing energy demand.

Exxon's (NYSE:XOM) 2050 emissions forecast is nearly 4% higher than in last year's outlook, as more coal is used to support power from wind and solar, and slowing electric vehicle sales in much of the world maintain high levels of oil demand.

The report sees oil demand peaking around 2030, then plateauing and remaining above 100M bbl/day through 2050, while raising its forecast for natural gas demand, which it sees increasing more than 20% by 2050 from last year's level due to rising global power use.

Combined oil and gas will account for 55% of the global energy mix in 25 years, down just a percentage point from 2024 levels, while coal and bioenergy comprise 14% and 10% of the global energy mix, respectively, the company said.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:XLE), (NYSEARCA:XOP), (AMLP), (VDE), (OIH), (IXC), (CRAK)

data.title: Gold extends gains on rate cut optimism, weaker U.S. dollar
data.description:
Gold futures gained Thursday on rising expectations of a September U.S. interest rate cut, adding to steady gains for the metal since Federal Reserve Chair Powell's speech last Friday that opened the door to easing monetary policy.

Markets are now pricing in a more than 87% chance of a 25 basis point rate cut at the Fed's September meeting, according to the CME FedWatch tool.

Investors also are watching the weaker U.S. dollar, with the dollar index falling 0.5% on Thursday, making greenback-priced gold more affordable for overseas buyers, and well as Friday's release of data on Personal Consumption Expenditures, a key inflation gauge for the central bank.

Concerns over the Fed's independence from the White House following President Trump's move to oust Governor Lisa Cook also are aiding gold's rally; in the latest development, Cook filed a lawsuit claiming Trump has no power to remove her from office, setting up a lengthy legal battle.

Analysts at Bank of America said they expect gold's rally to continue, maintaining their forecast for the metal to reach $4,000/oz by next year's H1, underpinned by falling interest rates and a weaker dollar.

"Potential rate cuts amid increasing inflation create fertile ground for dollar depreciation," BofA wrote in a report. "Rate cuts in an environment of continued elevated inflation would, in all likelihood, push the precious metal higher."

Gold posted its third straight daily gain, with the new Comex September front-month contract (XAUUSD:CUR) closing +0.7% to $3,431.80/oz, its highest settlement value in nearly three weeks, and the new Comex September front-month silver contract (XAGUSD:CUR) ended +1.2% to $39.190/bbl, its best settlement value since July 23.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (NYSEARCA:IAU), (NYSEARCA:NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Brown-Forman reaffirms low single-digit organic net sales decline guidance for fiscal 2026 amid U.S. distributor transition and emerging market gains
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.B) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Lawson E. Whiting recognized CFO Leanne Cunningham's planned retirement, stating she "has made the decision to retire on May 1, 2026" and praised her "impact...on our business results and culture." Whiting highlighted that "reported net sales declined 3%, but organic net sales increased 1% after adjusting for the A&D impact related to Sonoma-Cutrer, Finlandia and Korbel as well as the negative effect of foreign exchange." Organic net sales growth was led by emerging international markets, particularly Mexico and Brazil, while developed markets and the U.S. saw declines. Whiting pointed out, "our organic net sales increased 22%" in Mexico and "Brazil is another key growth driver...where organic net sales grew 30%." He noted the launch of Jack Daniel's Tennessee Blackberry in the U.S. and described the recent U.S. distributor transitions as the "first time in 60 years we had made a significant change to our distributor partners." Whiting said, "we're pleased with the early signs from our distributors and believe these changes will unlock growth."

  • Whiting addressed business headwinds, including "organic net sales for used barrels decreased over 40%" and "Canada's organic net sales declined nearly 60% as beverage alcohol products produced in the United States remained off the shelves." He commented on the strong reception to Jack Daniel's Tennessee Blackberry: "Distributors are excited with shipments already exceeding our expectations."

  • CFO Leanne D. Cunningham reported, "our reported gross profit decreased 2%, resulting in a reported gross margin of 59.8%. Our gross profit margin expanded 40 basis points." She stated, "organic advertising expense decreased 3%" and "organic SG&A investment decreased 7%, which reflected lower compensation-related expenses related to our workforce restructuring initiative." Cunningham shared, "reported operating income decreased 7% and organic operating income increased 2%...These results led to a 13% diluted earnings per share decrease to $0.36 per share."

Outlook

  • Cunningham reaffirmed full year fiscal 2026 guidance, stating, "we are forecasting organic operating income to decline in the low single-digit range" and "for fiscal 2026...we expect a low single-digit decline in organic net sales and reported gross margin expansion." She added, "our guidance assumes the current tariff impact on our products will remain unchanged."

  • The company expects "continued growth in our emerging markets and the depletion-based trends in the U.S. and developed international markets to remain similar to fiscal 2025 with the exception of Canada."

  • Capital expenditures are projected at "$125 million to $135 million for the fiscal year" and the effective tax rate is expected to be "in the range of approximately 21% to 23%."

Financial Results

  • Brown-Forman reported a 3% decline in reported net sales and a 1% increase in organic net sales. Gross margin was 59.8% with a 40 basis point expansion. Operating income fell 7% on a reported basis but rose 2% organically. Diluted earnings per share decreased 13% to $0.36 per share.

  • Organic advertising expense was down 3%, and SG&A dropped 7% due to workforce restructuring. Used barrel sales were described as a notable headwind, decreasing over 40%. Capital expenditures were lower than previous years.

Q&A

  • Peter K. Grom, UBS: Asked for clarification on distributor inventory impact and shipment trends. Cunningham responded, "for the entire fiscal year, we're expecting shipments and depletions to be in line," and detailed that "for full year, shipments and depletions in line with each other."

  • Nadine Sarwat, Bernstein: Inquired about U.S. trends and whether industry weakness is structural or cyclical. Whiting provided a detailed response, emphasizing, "I truly do not believe that the structural things that people talk about the most...just does not tank a market that quickly," attributing most of the slowdown to cyclical factors.

  • Andrea Faria Teixeira, JPMorgan: Asked about Jack Daniel's brand health and attracting new consumers. Whiting said, "we have really changed up our consumer communications...with our new campaign, that's what makes Jack, Jack. And I think it's been very successful."

  • Andrea Pistacchi, BofA: Questioned the launch and potential of Jack Daniel's Tennessee Blackberry. Cunningham answered, "we are off to a really strong start...there is a significant amount of excitement in the system from our distributors" but noted it is too early for depletion-based data.

  • Filippo Falorni, Citi: Sought details on gross margin outlook. Cunningham replied, "we're going to continue to benefit from the absence of Finlandia and the Sonoma-Cutrer...we do believe our price mix will largely offset our cost."

  • Eric Adam Serotta, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the competitive environment. Whiting responded, "pricing has continued to be...fairly rational. Tequila...seems to be the most aggressive for obvious reasons. But American whiskey is holding up."

  • Kevin Michael Grundy, BNP Paribas: Inquired about growth strategy and emerging markets. Whiting stated, "we are naturally going to allocate more resources into these emerging markets...there is a meaningful opportunity there."

  • Bonnie Lee Herzog, Goldman Sachs: Asked about EBIT impact from shipment loads and price/mix drivers. Cunningham referred to provided schedules for EBIT impact and cited "higher growth of New Mix and the lower used barrel sales" as price/mix factors.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on the sustainability of shipment-driven growth, the structural versus cyclical debate, and the competitive landscape, often with a neutral to slightly negative tone. Questions focused on shipment phasing, U.S. market weakness, and long-term category health.

  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in prepared remarks and the Q&A, with Whiting stating, "we're pleased with the start to our fiscal 2026," and Cunningham emphasizing, "our first quarter was a good start to fiscal 2026." The tone was consistent with the previous quarter, showing calm amid persistent headwinds.

  • Compared to the prior quarter, the level of skepticism from analysts remained elevated, especially regarding the U.S. market and shipment trends. Management's tone showed continued confidence, mirroring the previous call's mix of realism and optimism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reaffirmed its guidance for a low single-digit decline in organic net sales, consistent with the previous quarter's outlook. Shipment phasing linked to distributor transitions was cited as a temporary factor, with management expecting normalization by the second half of the year.

  • Strategic focus shifted further toward emerging markets and innovation, including the Jack Daniel's Tennessee Blackberry launch and U.S. distributor restructuring. The previous quarter also emphasized these areas, but this call provided more detail on execution and early results.

  • Analysts continued to probe the structural versus cyclical debate, with management reiterating its view that cyclical factors dominate current headwinds.

  • Key financial metrics, including margin expansion, cost controls, and capital expenditure, remained in line with previous guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited volatility in the operating environment, elevated input costs, and lower used barrel sales as significant headwinds. The ongoing trade dispute impacting Canadian sales and the risk of further tariff disruptions were specifically mentioned.

  • The company is mitigating risks through workforce restructuring, streamlined cost controls, and enhanced distributor partnerships. Management also referenced ongoing monitoring of inventory levels and shipment phasing.

  • Analysts expressed concern about the sustainability of shipment-driven sales, pressure on U.S. growth, and the potential for further competitive pricing activity.

Final Takeaway

Brown-Forman began fiscal 2026 with modest organic growth in emerging markets and continued challenges in developed markets and the U.S., driven by economic headwinds and distributor transitions. The company reaffirmed its guidance for a low single-digit decline in organic net sales and expects shipment trends to normalize in the second half of the year. Management remains focused on strategic innovation, particularly the launch of Jack Daniel's Tennessee Blackberry, and is leveraging new distributor relationships to drive future growth. Cost reductions from workforce restructuring and a streamlined organization are expected to support margins. Brown-Forman's leadership maintains confidence in its portfolio and long-term prospects, despite ongoing volatility and market uncertainties.
